speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking about the establishment of the ghetto, very late in 1941 in Lwow. How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the times exactly-- one never thought of the months there. There was no time. Everything was timeless, so I don't-- I can't remember exactly which months. I don't think that anyone remembers exactly the months, because people didn't think of it. I never had a date. I never knew what day-- I didn't know it's Monday, Sunday, or what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about going to your sister's place to check how she and her family were doing. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to the ghetto, yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before. What happened after you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we went to the ghetto? We didn't stay long. Because after the-- we were afraid. We thought perhaps in a ghetto, it will be easier, because they took the children. They went from house to house. And even non-Jews showed them when they came around to where Jews live-- Poles and Ukrainians. And my sister lived on a street, the same thing. There were not many Jews. That was apart from Lwow that-- they were a bit wealthier. And like here, there are some streets-- Turek once-- later on, Jews came to Turek here. But Turek and [INAUDIBLE], not many Jews lived there in the beginning, until later on. And Jews lived more like now. We call it-- like the Jewish ghetto, it was [INAUDIBLE] with synagogues, most old synagogues are there, and a lot of Jews lived there. That was the same was there in Lwow. We lived in a part where not many Jews lived, so we went later to the ghetto. But people nearer-- and it was like a ghetto. As matter of fact, I had some cousins there living there, too. And that was [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], I remember all the streets there. And there were most concentrated with Jewish people, Jewish houses. I don't know if there lived any non-Jews. Mostly Jews. But it wasn't a closed ghetto. It was open. It wasn't a ghetto. But Jews gathered there together. And there was a market. And there were little synagogues. And not far from there that was [INAUDIBLE] And you called it [INAUDIBLE] It was a big part there. And there were, because there lived only Jews, the Germans established the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They fenced it around. It was a huge place, fenced around. And before you get-- and a gate to go in. Before you went there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the street, what was still [INAUDIBLE] On one side of the street, there was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fenced onto-- the same like this, but before the ghetto. And the Germans had their buildings there, the Gestapo, with the door open. And as we went down-- so we went down together, my, myself with my child, my sister. I don't know where her husband-- he didn't go with us. Maybe he went later. I don't remember seeing him. Could be that he went a little later. And my younger sister, who survived, and lived in France now, whom I found 22 years after the war, I didn't know that she's alive. I just traveled from here to New York, to United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's another story. I'll say later about it. Though she was with us. And we had to go-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a big group of people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with children. And pass near on the right hand side, before going to-- on the way to the ghetto, we had to pass that buildings-- those buildings with the Germans, with the door open. And they looked through as we went in. They didn't want old people at the ghetto, only younger people, because later they took them out for work. As we passed there, two Gestapo men stood at the front and looked through. Exactly they could see it. Where was an older person. They took her out into that open door. Over there was a building. They shot them there. We could hear shootings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister-- I was in the back just near them as they passed. But I was then young with my little child. She was next, although she was not much older, still young. But older than I was. And knowing that she was a little bit older than I was, she was afraid that maybe they'd take her. They didn't take her. She was too young for that. And she told-- said me-- to me, would you change with me? You go to my place, and I will go to the-- I wasn't afraid. I didn't even know what it is. I said, yes. Why not? So we changed. And she went in front. Because near them, she could pass. They looked further for older ones. They got up quite a number of older people, men and women, and got them in there and killed them there. And we went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were a few buildings, not too many [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from this-- from before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- there was established-- I mean, Germans established it-- a Judenrat, like a committee, who communicated with them, the Germans, on our behalf. And that committee gave us lodgings, rooms, for people that they came. They didn't know where to go, what to do there. But it was quite big. It was huge. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who had money to pay for, for a better room paid it. I had nothing, neither my sister. So they put us in a little room without, without a roof, or a broken roof. If it rained, it rained on us. If it snowed, it snowed on us. But so we were there. There were no beds, nothing, not even a table, some benches. And we sat at night, and we slept on those benches, sitting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know if somewhere else there were ghettos in better conditions. It was very bad in ours, couldn't be worse. As I was there, that was about two weeks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Around the second week-- I had my child in my arms always. But she was not comfortable to sleep like that. She was undernourished, but still healthy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Not far from me, there was stuck out a little-- from a-- it was maybe once a big stove something. But it was from stone, not wooden. And I put something on top and laid her down there. So I thought she would be more comfortable to sleep there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I thought she sleeping. Had a look, she's sleeping. But I probably fell asleep sitting on that bench, near her, near. They were long benches on that bench. I think they put them in especially for people without beds, without anything. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought she sleeping. But in the early morning, I had a look. She was covered with snow. I slept. It started to snow. She was covered with snow, her face and everything. I got her up. And she couldn't open her mouth. She could-- she didn't breathe. And I thought, oh, what happened now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to go to see a doctor. Something happened. She couldn't open her mouth. And I knew straightaway, being a nurse-- I had a look here and there. And it was diphtheria. And it was around, diphtheria. But I didn't know about it. Must have been all, all illnesses were around. There was cholera, too. And she had diphtheria. I went down. That was in the half a floor, second floor. Downstairs was a better room, because we were on top, so they were covered. I went down. And there was a doctor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I worked at the Russian's laboratory, there was a man, [? Yebshenitz ?]. He came from-- but because he worked there, they didn't send him to Russia, deep to Siberia. And he was there. I was Mehr. And he was [? Mehrer. ?] We knew each other. I came over. And he was there. And I said, listen, have a look at my child. And I'm sure she has diphtheria.

speaker: interviewer
text: At one look, he couldn't look in-- open her mouth. He said, yes. I said, what can I do? He said, nothing. What can you do? They take children here, children that they survived. They couldn't get them from the houses there before, they took them out in the ghetto. They will kill her all the same. What's the-- what's the difference? If she dies, that will be a natural death. She's sick and she will die. And tomorrow, or after tomorrow, they will take children and cut them into pieces or, or throw them to the wall, as they did, cut their heads open. So what do you worry about? It's all the same. I said, what? You, you're a doctor, how can you say anything like it? Have you got injection for anti-diphtheria? I have only one. And I keep it for myself. I said, if you are adult, first of all, you'll not have-- get diphtheria. And how do you know? You have one inject-- one is left only. I said, give it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't give it to you, because I keep it for myself. I said, listen. If you don't give it to her, I don't know what I will do. You know I'm not normal now. I will grab anything, and I'll kill you. I'll kill you here. Nobody will help you. If they hear, they can't help you. I got crazy. I was not normal. And I started to hit my head against the wall. I said, I still don't know, maybe my husband will come back after the war, after it's over. We don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he will say, you left our-- you let our child die? How can you do a thing like that? He said, I can see that you're not normal. Have it. He gave me the last injection. He gave it to her. He gave her the injection.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, listen, now one thing. I give her the injection, but she must go to a hospital. She must go. Without the hospital, she will die even if she has-- the injection won't help. She has to go to a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, all right. So I'll took her-- take her to a hospital. He said, which hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Infectious hospital is miles away from here. And it is war time. There were around-- people were talking, even if a doctor goes out at nighttime, they shot him on the street. So it's impossible. You can't reach that hospital. It is like from here further than to Carlton, much further, twice in to Carlton from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you go there? It's-- on the streets, it's dark. Windows had to be darkened everywhere. No lights there. You know where it is? I said, I remember, yes. When I did nursing, I remember we went once a week to the infectious for lectures there. And I remember that hospital. Do you know how to go there? No, I don't know. We went there by cars. They took us there, the whole group of nursing from one hospital to another. But I don't remember now. With a child, how can you dare? How can you go? That's just no one would do it. And if you go, you're surely crazy, because as soon as you go out on the streets, they will shot-- straightaway, you'll be shot. So I said, all right. So at least they will not take away my child. I don't let her die. They will kill us, be shot both on the street. All right, I'm going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't go. Please, don't go. Don't do it. Because it's imposs-- you can't-- you can't do it. No one in the world would do a thing like that. I said-- my sister was there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My younger sister was there, not the brother because he went to Russia. And my sister said, no, we won't let you go. We won't let-- here, we are together. What will happen tomorrow will happen to us all together. But to let you out of something-- it was about 11 or 12 o'clock midnight. How can you-- what are you think-- you don't think. You're not normal. I said, because I'm not normal, I'm going. I took from someone a cover, just a cover like the, the peasants. We had to wear the-- on our left arm, we had to wear a white band with a [INAUDIBLE], blue [INAUDIBLE] that all Jews must have. If they found a Jew without it they shot him or they tortured him. I put that in underneath. I still was not that-- assumed Christian. I was still Jewish from the ghetto, before it happened, before. I put it on, covered like that, took my child. She had on her tiny little feet, little tiny red boots. For her first birthday, my sister bought it for her and gave it to her, somewhere for a lot of money, because it was at the Russians. She was 139. She had them on naked little feet. And was nearly naked, and I grabbed her underneath me and held her to myself like this, and wanted to go out. They grabbed me all. They hold me. They didn't let me go. I said, let me go. We'll die all together. It doesn't matter, here or there. At least I'm trying. I'll do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's just-- I thought myself, is it possible to do it or not? Middle of the night and go to that hospital, it was like to another city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, they thought they can't do anything with me. They let me. Didn't even say goodbye. I just wanted to get out of there with my child. I went out. I went out from that building through the gate, through the front gate. As I came there, two Jewish boys stood on both sides at the gate, kapo, two kapos, [INAUDIBLE] the Germans. If they-- that was known. If they let out a person, day or night, from the ghetto-- they count the heads. Every day they came in. They count the people. If one is missing, they kill one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I came to the gate with the child covered like that, they spoke to me in Yiddish. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] I said, I go out to a hospital. The child has diphtheria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Diphtheria, shmiphtheria. You're not going out. We won't let you out here. Go back, go back into your room. And you're not going, because we won't let you out. I didn't argue. I said, all right. I went out. I didn't go back. On the other side, there was a big fence. I think there were a few kilometers, the fence. Right down-- I didn't know what is it. It went down the other side. There were little country-- I don't know [NON-ENGLISH], how they call it. A few houses, country houses. It wasn't any more the city. It was the side of the city where I was. I went down. And it was cold, wind, wet. There was no even-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to walk, it was not paved. And because of the rain always, it was very soft soil, you know, bloto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mud.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in and out with my shoes, out, and I went. And I said, where am I going? I want to go to the hospital, I thought. And I thought-- I dont know. I think that's why I have such a good memory, because I had to think all the time then. I'm going-- I told myself, I'm going to the hospital, just see what you'll do.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was a fence. How to get out with a child? Finally, I came to the fence. It was a rotten, wooden fence. And between the fence was broken. There were holes inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took my child. I couldn't go-- it was high underneath. I threw her out even though there were puddles of water. I threw her out. And I after her out there through the hole. And I came to the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: I picked her up and said, and said, now where to go? And then I started think, no, it must to the left side. Started to walk, not knowing where, but I felt. I guess my intuition got me over there. And I started to walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked a long time. I walked around. No one was there. I thought maybe I'll find someone that, you know-- say I'll be from a ghetto. Sure, I'm Jewish. But no one was there. And I walked, no pavement at all. It was very hard from step to step. I walked, and I walked along. Suddenly as I walked, I had a look to my side. As I was-- people think, she made it up. But I can see it now. I believe it. I don't know what it was. I don't believe in miracles. My life there during the war was all miracles if there are some miracles. I've seen a big dog at the side of my child. I asked later people, asked the rabbi here about it. He said, that's possible. See, he interpreted it differently. And I interpret differently. A huge dog, like a calf. A black, shiny dog with golden eyes, yellow eyes. Maybe dogs have yellow eyes. I don't know. I never looked into the eyes of dogs. Yellow eyes, and he looked up to me. And was at the child's-- like-- like just at the side of my child, like protecting her, near my child. And that dog, a huge dog walked with me. I wasn't afraid. He didn't bark. He just went step by step and looking at me and looking at the side of my child where I held her in my arms. And suddenly she started-- she hit me in my stomach with the legs, the leg, one leg. She hit me. She couldn't talk. And I looked down, right where she hit me, I found a boot fell down, her little boot. And I was very happy then, because she lived. Because all the time she was like dead, nearly cold in my arms. That means that the injection works. If she could-- she couldn't talk. She couldn't open her mouth, because of the diphtheria, all infected inside, her throat, her mouth. And as she hit me, she let me know that her boot fell down. And I got it to her, and I put it on. And that dog was walking with me until we came to the border of the city, because that was outside the city. That was not the city, not Lwow itself. I don't know where it was there. But I know it was a few peasant houses. And that's where the dog came from. It must've been a big dog. But I can't understand it. Till now, I think of it. And he brought me out right to the entrance to the city. And then I had a look. He disappeared. They would have shot him if he would come to the city, if someone would see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were the Germans patrolling. You know, patrols? [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know how you could-- and I walked. I started to walk. Where to walk, which side. I just-- not knowing the streets, not knowing exactly where it is, because I didn't live in that part. I didn't know it at all. It was huge. It was big, very big.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to walk, and big steps. And I was quicker and quicker and quicker. And I walked. And I came somewhere, maybe it was not far, it was a street-- I forgot it. I recall it. I know I knew it. I'll recall it later. I went out 10 o'clock, 11 o'clock, about 11 o'clock at night time. Started to walk. It was past 11:00. It was 5 o'clock in the morning. All night I walked. I haven't seen one German, one soldier on the street. And they said, people warned, don't go out, because they're everywhere. They're patrolling the streets. It's war time, everywhere. No one. I haven't seen any one. No, I was alone in the street in a war. Like someone just helped me to get out of there, or someone was behind me. Someone from up looked after me. I wasn't a very-- believer, you know. I couldn't believe it. But I know something happened. Something-- I went and there was not one-- not speaking of Gestapos, not a soldier, no one was on the street. It was dark. And I walked and I walked. And I didn't look right or left. I looked always before me. 5 o'clock in the morning, I think it was about. It was already light. Maybe, maybe later, I came to the gates of that hospital, not knowing exactly how to go there. And I came to the gate to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: I opened the gate. It was not locked. I came in. There was a-- you know? How do you call them? Before important buildings, there's always patrol, patrol or what. Sentry. Sentry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a German in a helmet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said, halt. So I stopped. I stood there just before the gate. And he was about a few meters from me there before-- near the hospital. So I stopped. The hospitals were-- they were Polish ones. They were all Ukrainian. The Ukrainians took over. So I stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] Where do you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to speak German because for them, I didn't know German. Jews, they knew-- Jews knew German, know German, or they knew then. I said, Kind, Kind, Kind. I always talked like that in the prison, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Typhus, typhus. He went back. For typhus, they were all scared, all afraid. I didn't even say anything else. Typhus. Typhus, typhus, typhus. He went back, went in. He was scared. He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] Ukraine, Ukraine. Ah, Ukraine, [SPEAKING GERMAN] He showed me, because I told him I'm Ukraine, so I'm your friend. So I was dressed like a poor Ukrainian, peasant woman. Said, [GERMAN] He showed me exactly where to go to the site of the hospital for-- infectious site. I went there, and straightaway I found it. As I came-- oh, and it was a huge building, infectious. Open the door with the child. I didn't know what I'm doing. But I was still-- I waited-- I don't know, I would have fainted before. But I wanted to reach the hospital. Open the door. It was-- I can remember exactly-- stony floor, those squares, stone. There were a few steps to go in. I still went up [INAUDIBLE] the steps. When I came to the hall, suddenly I fell with my child to the floor and fainted. It was too much. It was so much. I fainted. I don't know anything because I fainted. I didn't know anything. I woke up. I didn't have the child. I was in a room, inside somewhere there on a bed lying. And a Ukrainian sister, with the black, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came up to me. Third thing what happened to me, I didn't have anymore my cover. And I had a-- I felt here. She said, no, we burned it. I was lucky always. I was lucky. We burned it. I said, where is my child in Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, she's in the other side of the hospital. Will she live? And she looked up to the sky. [NON-ENGLISH] one-- in the Ukraine, one [INAUDIBLE] knows. I said, please help her. I give her to the Holy Maria. If she survive, I don't-- I don't think I will-- if she survive, give her, take her, let her live and take her wherever you want to. Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
